elements.sent-2411-001: In this work, we develop an analytical framework that integrates opinion dynamics with a recommendation system.
elements.sent-2411-002: By incorporating elements such as collaborative filtering, we provide a precise characterization of how recommendation systems shape interpersonal interactions and influence opinion formation.
elements.sent-2411-003: Moreover, the property of the coevolution of both opinion dynamics and recommendation systems is also shown.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, the convergence of this coevolutionary system is theoretically proved, and the mechanisms behind filter bubble formation are elucidated.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our analysis of the maximum number of opinion clusters shows how recommendation system parameters affect opinion grouping and polarization.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we incorporate the influence of propagators into our model and propose a reinforcement learning-based solution.
elements.sent-2411-007: The analysis and the propagation solution are demonstrated in simulation using the Yelp data set.
elements.sent-2411-008: The evolution of human opinion is always a hot topic from ancient times to the present, such that the governors can understand social influence, predict social behavior and enhance decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the era of traditional communication without the Internet, people primarily formed connections through geographical proximity and familial ties.
elements.sent-2411-010: This relationship is typically stable and seldom changed, such that the evolution of human opinions can be regarded as based on a fixed communication network in the past studies.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, with the rise of the internet and social media, the establishment and breaking of the connection has become much easier than in the past, leading to highly dynamic networks \cite{kwak_fragile_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, individuals' opinions also significantly influent the change of connections.
elements.sent-2411-013: Specifically, two individuals with similar opinions are easier to get connected online, while two with opposite opinions may dissolve the existing connection\cite{cinelli_echo_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this context, the dynamics of online opinions are, in fact, a process of co-evolution between opinions and the network itself.
elements.sent-2411-015: Since online interaction currently dominates most of people’s social lives, studying the co-evolutionary process of opinion and network is of great importance.
elements.sent-2411-016: The opinion dynamics are widely studied based on a fixed network, where the evolution of individual opinion over time in the static communication topology is explored.
elements.sent-2411-017: To describe such opinion dynamics, some foundational models, such as the DeGroot model \cite{degroot_reaching_1974 and the other models \cite{french_jr_formal_1956, p_mathematical_1964, friedkin_social_1990, taylor_towards_1968 , provide valuable insights into the mechanisms of opinion dissemination and social influence.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Considering the dynamic social networks resulting from the possible connection shift between individuals, opinion propagation within time-varying networks are also explored \cite{li_opinion_2018, matveev_stability_2013, moreau_stability_2005, munz_consensus_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, these works neglect the impact of opinions on the network changes.
elements.sent-2411-020: The Bounded Confidence Model \cite{krause_discrete_2000 is one the most well-known co-evolution models, stipulating that individuals are entirely insensitive to opinions outside their confidence interval.}
elements.sent-2411-021: This implies that the graph depends specifically on the mutual distances between individuals, co-evolving with their opinions.
elements.sent-2411-022: Other notable models exploring co-evolving opinion dynamics include \cite{burger_kinetic_2021, gu_co-evolution_2017, kang_coevolution_2022, liu_emergence_2023, liu_interplay_2021, liu_modeling_2024, rossi_asynchronous_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The coevolutionary models contribute a deep and detailed understanding of opinion change by considering the adaptability of social connections.
elements.sent-2411-024: Despite such advancements, most coevolutionary models still fall short of capturing the complexity of modern online social networks, where the recommendation system plays an important role \cite{berjani2011recommendation .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Recommendation systems, mediating a large share of current online interactions, are widely used in online platforms like social media \cite{anandhan2018social , e-commerce \cite{hussien2021recommendation , and streaming services \cite{hasan2018excessive .}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: By using algorithms for suggesting content, products, or connections based on user opinions, recommendation systems shape the connection between individuals and influence the structure of networks \cite{isinkaye2015recommendation .}
elements.sent-2411-027: While they streamline the connection formation process for individuals, the recommendation systems may induce ``filter bubbles'' \cite{nguyen2014exploring and ``echo chambers'' \cite{ge2020understanding .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Specifically, users are more frequently exposed to others that align with their preexisting opinions, reinforcing opinions and limiting exposure to diverse perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-029: The influence of recommendation systems on both the formation of new connections and the reinforcement of existing ones adds complexity to opinion dynamics studies.
elements.sent-2411-030: This makes the traditional coevolutionary models unsuitable for opinion evolution within modern online social networks with recommendation systems.
elements.sent-2411-031: Currently, only limited studies specifically explore the impact of recommendation systems on public opinion.
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, \cite{stoica_algorithmic_2018, bellina_effect_2023, chaney_how_2018, piao_humanai_2023, vendeville_opening_2023 investigate the indirect impact of the recommended items on the change of human opinions, but they did not consider the formation and evolution of group opinions from the perspective of opinion dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Some have recently attempted to explore opinion dynamics in the context of recommendation systems \cite{cinus_effect_2022, perra_modelling_2019, pescetelli_indirect_2022, ramaciotti_morales_auditing_2021, santos_link_2021, tornberg_how_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, these works only provide empirical results via simulations and lack comprehensive analysis of the underlying theoretical principle.
elements.sent-2411-035: The theoretical analysis of opinion dynamics in systems with recommendation mechanisms is conducted in \cite{rossi_closed_2022 , where the authors investigate a tractable analytical model of users interacting with an online news aggregator, aiming to elucidate the feedback loop between the evolution of user opinions and personalized content recommendations.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Similarly, \cite{iannelli_filter_2022 explores a multi-state voter model, where users interact with “personalized information” that reflects their historically dominant opinions, with a given probability.}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, in both studies \cite{rossi_closed_2022, iannelli_filter_2022 , the recommendation system is employed indirectly in news stories or election options, rather than directly in opinions from individuals in a social network.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Therefore, the analysis of the co-evolution of interpersonal networks is excluded in their works.
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, substantial research focuses on modeling and predicting opinion dynamics within systems with recommendation algorithms, overlooking the potential influence exerted by propagators to steer public opinion.
elements.sent-2411-040: In this work, we propose an analytical framework combined with opinion dynamics and a recommendation system.
elements.sent-2411-041: We provide a precise characterization of how recommendation systems influence interpersonal networks, accommodating complex behaviors such as collaborative filtering.
elements.sent-2411-042: Theoretically, we prove the convergence of this complex system, offering an explanation for the formation of filter bubbles.
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, by establishing a relationship between the upper bound on the number of opinion clusters and the solution to the sphere-packing problem, we demonstrate how recommendation system parameters impact opinion grouping and polarization.
elements.sent-2411-044: Moreover, the role of propagators is incorporated into the proposed framework, to shape group opinions.
elements.sent-2411-045: Specifically, the propagation behavior from propagators is regarded as an optimization problem, which minimizes the influence cost while achieving the formation of the predefined group opinions.
elements.sent-2411-046: To solve such a problem within the proposed framework with opinion dynamics in recommendation systems, i.e., a co-evolving, complex system, mechanism-based system, a reinforcement learning-based controller is employed.
elements.sent-2411-047: Furthermore, the effectiveness of the proposed framework and the controller is demonstrated via simulations, where the feasibility of controlling opinion propagation within the framework is shown.

elements.sent-2411-217: In this subsection, the effectiveness of the reinforcement learning-based control strategy is demonstrated by using the Yelp data set \cite{_yelp_ , which consists of $8$ users and $3$-dimensional opinions.}
elements.sent-2411-218: To shape the users' opinion, $2$ propagators are introduced, whose opinions are generated via reinforcement learning algorithm and not affected by the users.
elements.sent-2411-219: The target opinion is chosen as $\bm{x ^c = [0.8147, 0.9058, 0.1270]^T$, and control horizon is set as $20$ steps.}
elements.sent-2411-220: The threshold $\epsilon$ in \eqref{f_bar and \eqref{g_bar is chosen as $0.2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: The structures of the critic and actor networks are shown in \cref{fig_network_structure .}
elements.sent-2411-222: Specifically, the concatenated system state $\bm{X \in \mathbb{R ^{8 \times 3}$ is reshaped into a $24$-dimensional vector, which combines with the target opinion $\bm{x ^c \in \mathbb{R ^3$ and serves as the $27$-dimensional input for critic and actor networks.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: The critic network, which generates the prediction of the cumulative reward with dimension $1$, has $1$ hidden layers, which applies a fully connected layer with $256$ hidden nodes and ReLU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-224: The actor network returns the concatenated mean prediction and its deviation, whose common layer consists of a fully connected layer with $256$ hidden nodes and is activated by ReLU function.
elements.sent-2411-225: The $6$-dimensional output of the common layer is the result of a fully connected layer, which serves as the input for the mean and variance networks.
elements.sent-2411-226: Both network components for mean and variance are composed of a ReLU layer, followed by a fully connected layer with $6$-dimensional output.
elements.sent-2411-227: While the Softplus function is chosen for variance, the sigmoid layer is used for mean, such that the control command belongs to $(0,1)^6$.
elements.sent-2411-228: The generated control command will reshape to two $3$-dimensional vectors $\bm{u _1$ and $\bm{u _2$ in \eqref{discrete model .}}}

elements.sent-2411-236: In this paper, a framework combining opinion dynamics and a recommendation system is proposed, i.e., the ODRS framework, where the connection between individuals follows either distance- or angle-based method.
elements.sent-2411-237: The proposed framework is analytical, where the convergence and clustering properties are formally proven.
elements.sent-2411-238: Moreover, the ODRS framework allows the introduction of external effectors, i.e., propagators, whose behavior is modeled as an optimal control problem to shape users' opinions.
elements.sent-2411-239: Due to the high nonlinearity of the proposed framework, the optimal propagation strategy is obtained using reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-240: The properties of the ODRS framework and the effectiveness of the reinforcement learning-based opinion-shaping strategy are demonstrated via simulations with the actual Yelp data set.

elements.sent-2411-253: The evolution of human opinion is always a hot topic from ancient times to the present, such that the governors can understand social influence, predict social behavior and enhance policy-making.
elements.sent-2411-254: With the emergence of the internet and big data, the evolution of human opinion has undergone significant changes, thereby attracting increasing attention from researchers.
elements.sent-2411-255: This relationship is typically stable and seldom changed, such that the evolution of human opinions can be regarded as based on a fixed communication network in past studies.
elements.sent-2411-256: However, with the rise of the internet and social media, the establishment and breaking of the connection has become much easier than in the past, leading to highly dynamic networks \cite{kwak_fragile_2011 which then influences the update of opinions \cite{del_vicario_echo_2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Specifically, two individuals with similar opinions are easier to get connected online, while two with opposite opinions may dissolve the existing connection \cite{cinelli_echo_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-258: In this context, the dynamics of online opinions is, in fact, a process of co-evolution between opinions and the network.
elements.sent-2411-259: Since online interaction currently dominates most people’s social lives, studying the co-evolutionary process of opinion and network is of great importance.
elements.sent-2411-260: The opinion dynamics are widely studied based on a fixed network, where the evolution of individual opinion in the static communication topology is explored.
elements.sent-2411-261: To describe such opinion dynamics, some foundational models \cite{degroot_reaching_1974, french_jr_formal_1956, friedkin_social_1990 provide valuable insights into the mechanisms of opinion propagation and social influence.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Considering the dynamic social networks resulting from the possible connection shift between individuals, opinion propagation within time-varying networks is also explored \cite{moreau_stability_2005, munz_consensus_2011, he_reinforcement-learning-based_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-263: The Bounded Confidence Model \cite{krause_discrete_2000 , \cite{lorenz_continuous_2007-2 is a co-evolutionary framework, in which agents ignore opinions outside their confidence interval.}}
elements.sent-2411-264: Consequently, the interaction graph is determined by pairwise distance metrics in opinion space and co-evolves with the opinions of all agents.
elements.sent-2411-265: Other notable models exploring co-evolving opinion dynamics include \cite{gu_co-evolution_2017, kang_coevolution_2022, liu_emergence_2023, liu_modeling_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-266: Recommendation systems, mediating a large share of current online interactions, are widely used in online platforms like social media, e-commerce and streaming services.
elements.sent-2411-267: While they facilitate the connection formation process for individuals, the recommendation systems may induce ``filter bubbles'' and ``echo chambers''.
elements.sent-2411-268: Specifically, users are more frequently exposed to others who align with their preexisting opinions, reinforcing opinions and limiting exposure to diverse perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-269: For instance, \cite{bellina_effect_2023, piao_humanai_2023, vendeville_opening_2023 investigate the indirect impact of the recommended items on the change of opinions, but they did not consider the formation and evolution of group opinions from the perspective of opinion dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-270: However, these works only provide empirical results via simulations and lack a comprehensive analysis of the underlying theoretical principle.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, in both studies \cite{rossi_closed_2022, iannelli_filter_2022 , the recommendation system only provides content selection methods for e.g., news items, and neglects its effect on user–user connections.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Therefore, the analysis of the co-evolution of interpersonal networks is excluded from their works.

elements.sent-2411-277: Specifically, the propagation behavior is regarded as an optimization problem, which minimizes the influence cost while achieving the formation of the predefined group opinions.

elements.sent-2411-366: The next generation is produced using a combination of Gaussian mutation and uniform crossover.
elements.sent-2411-367: A tournament selection is employed to select elites as parents, which are then used to generate new offspring.

elements.sent-2411-375: In this paper, a framework combining opinion dynamics and a recommendation system is proposed, i.e., the ODRS framework, where the connection between individuals follows either a distance- or an angle-based method.
elements.sent-2411-376: Moreover, the ODRS framework allows the introduction of a controller, i.e., propagators, whose behavior is modeled as an optimal control problem to shape users' opinions.
elements.sent-2411-377: We believe that our work contributes to a better quantitative understanding of the societal impacts of recommendation algorithms, supports the fair application of such systems, and offers insights toward the broader goal of promoting responsible and ethical technology design.
